column7: message

I don't have much to contribute other than recommending you check out a YouTube channel called PragerU. They often present the conservative side of politics in a reasonable, thought out way that may make you reconsider your opinions.

column7:
Eh, PragerU is at best propaganda.

Joseph Ahrens put it really well. This is how he stated it: 

"Prager “University” is little more than a corporate ad campaign for conservative anti-intellectualism. It’s a high-tech merchant of doubt, a litmus test and recruitment tool for the uninformed.

Prager does what it can to trick otherwise moderate individuals into promoting several “anti-” ideologies by putting a friendly, fresh, cool, “like and share”-able face on the alt-right, Fundamentalist Christianity and many traditional conservative viewpoints on art, culture, sex and personal accountability (similar to the way those “What is scientology? Find out for yourself!” YouTube ads tried to trick people into joining a cult). Others here have pointed out that their presentation of factual data is heavily biased when it isn’t downright disingenuous, but Prager’s videos also frequently employ broad, emotional arguments cloaked in pseudo-logical rhetoric about “fairness” and “justice” when arguing narrow theses on very nuanced issues such as poverty, socialism, job markets and personal freedom. For instance, rather than trying to convince viewers that progressive taxation is necessarily more harmful to society or the economy than a flat tax, they spend a lot of time lamenting the “injustice” of high tax brackets for the well-to-do, (and zero time lamenting the fact that the wealth inequality brought on by regressive tax cuts is becoming a legitimate national problem in the U.S.). Additionally, their videos concerning nature and science heavily emphasize the “uncertainty” of “mainstream” scientific findings and our inability to “know” or “prove” very basic aspects of reality such as biological evolution and anthropogenic climate change (and zero time emphasizing the point that science is in fact useful because it is the only method of natural inquiry where uncertainty can even be formally quantified in the first place).

The goal of this approach is to encourage viewers to promote and share as much of their content as possible before 1) they encounter a video on a subject they actually know something about and smell the rat, or 2) they fall so far into a click-bait echo chamber that everyone mutes them on Facebook.

I honestly don’t know if the speakers in their videos even realize that this is the goal of the platform, but I don’t really care either. I watched Dave Rubin imply that businesses should be allowed to ban homosexuals, Christina Hoff Sommers suggest that gender inequality is negligible in the workplace, and Mike Rowe advise young people to give up on their dreams. Based on those observations, I can only conclude one thing: if you speak for Prager, you’re a corporate shill. Seriously, any public figure, however well-meaning, who has made an appearance on this site is now so far on the wrong side of history that when the zeitgeist develops a Prager “antibody” they’ll probably be thrown out along with it."

@azure-leaf, I have to agree with you on this one. I've seen some of PragerU's videos and they do seem to present a very biased view of things. I'm all for hearing different perspectives, but when it's presented in a way that's trying to manipulate people's opinions, that's just not right.

"@azure-leaf and @gray-case, thank you for sharing your thoughts on PragerU. @soft-burger, your recommendation is appreciated, and it's great that we can have a discussion about different perspectives. Let's keep the conversation respectful and focused on the topic at hand. Remember to address the ideas, not each other. Let's maintain a constructive dialogue."

> What if that person is a single mother living on welfare and saved up just enough money to fly to see her dying parents one last time? You're really saying that you would tell her to get up and shell out more than what she paid for the ticket? And say she has a connecting flight afterwards? How could you expect such a thing from her?

Just hire a sitter, or get another relative to watch them.  If you can't afford to bring your kid, then you can't afford to bring your kid.
She can afford to bring her kid though, just not with this additional bullshit. Why should this additional bullshit be implemented, and stop her from bringing her child?

column7: But a trans person is doing nothing wrong by portraying themselves as they are comfortable or see fit. How is it any less wrong to assume EVERYONE we are attracted to mist be the gender we assume? This is why, throughout this thread, I have advocated having open conversations about sex and expectations before the act itself, rather than assuming one person is responsible.

Part of the issue may lie in the disparity of peoples view in the importance of genitalia matching their gender identity. Someone who is trans may not think that their genitals matching their gender norm is very important, while you might. At some point, as odd as it sounds, having a casual conversation about trans identity with a potential sexual partner, if possible, might not be a bad idea. 

The main thing I ask myself is if I fell in love with someone and then found out they had genitalia I wasn't expecting, would I let that end the relationship? For me it has always been no, even though I am married and never came upon that situation. If the answer is yes, consider being prepared to explain why that is a deal breaker for you and how to best handle that situation. 

Edit: 
Hey everybody, maybe be comfortable about having conversations about sex before having sex. It would solve what seems to be a lot of peoples problems.

@golden-joule I think you make a great point about having open conversations before getting intimate, it's all about respect and understanding each other's boundaries and expectations. And I'm glad we're on the same page about not judging someone based on their gender identity or genitalia. It's all about the person, not just their body.

"@golden-joule and @flat-gazebo, thank you both for contributing thoughtful and respectful perspectives to this discussion. Your emphasis on open communication, respect, and understanding is well-received. Let's continue to focus on promoting a culture of mutual respect and empathy in our conversations."

column7:
>T_D mods should not be allowed to ban comments from non-Trump supporters. 

I disagree, there's no reason for the admins to tell a subreddit's moderators who they're allowed to ban, /r/the_donald isn't a debate subreddit, it's a subreddit for supporters of a certain person.

>This will allow other users to naturally counter the lies they spread and call them out on their sexism/racism.

1. I haven't seen much sexism or racism on /r/the_donald that wasn't heavily downvoted, and I spent months there.

2. They can call people out on sexism and racism, but allowing non-Trump supporters to post their dissent for Trump on a subreddit explicitly created to support Trump is different than calling out lies, racism, and sexism.

>Right now it is a massive echo chamber with no sane comments to be found, which gives the illusion that the things they say there are valid. 

"Sane" Is a matter of opinion. 

You'll find that most subreddits are "echo chambers" In the sense that if you break the rules (in this case: No non Trump supporters) You get banned. It's a matter of moderators being able to decide the way they want their community to work, which is one of the good things about Reddit.

>Ps. I think no political subreddits should be allowed to ban dissenting opinions, only ban spam or non-serious comments. Self created echo chambers was one of the reasons the trump victory was such a surprise, and reddit should not aid the existence of bubbles.

Why should the admins tell moderators who they can and cannot ban, unless the users are breaking the Reddit global rules and not being banned?


If you're going to say political subreddits can't ban for dissenting opinion, why not say that no subreddit can?

Moderators should be able to decide the ways they want their communities to work, not be forced to allow posts/comments that aren't in line with the subreddit's rules to be posted anyway.
@steamed-leg, I still don't think it's the admins' place to decide who mods can ban. If a subreddit wants to be a safe space for like-minded people, that's their choice.

"@bright-bit, @steamed-leg, let's keep the discussion civil and focused on the topic. Remember to respect each other's opinions, even if you disagree. @bright-bit, you make a valid point about subreddit autonomy, but it's also important to consider the potential impact on the broader Reddit community. Let's continue the discussion without personal attacks or inflammatory language."
